title: Jig
content:
The jig (,) is a form of lively folk dance in compound metre, as well as the accompanying dance tune. It first gained popularity in 16th-century Scotland and Northern England, and was quickly adopted on mainland Europe where it eventually became the final movement of the mature Baroque dance suite (the French gigue; Italian and Spanish giga). Today it is most associated with Irish dance music, Scottish country dance and the Métis people in Canada.

Jigs were originally in duple compound metre, (e.g., time), but have been adapted to a variety of time signature s, by which they are often classified into groups, including light jigs, slip jigs, single jigs, double jigs, and treble jigs. Origins. The term "jig" was probably derived from the French "giguer", meaning ‘to jump’ or the Italian "giga". The use of “jig” in Irish dance derives from the Irish "jigeánnai", itself borrowed from the Old English "giga" meaning ‘old dance’.
